what_it_does: A read-only scan report of /home/tim/company that documents the current state of hooks, events, the noticeboard surface, and firing mechanisms, and argues that hooks and triggers are distinct categories with a specific live-vs-missing gap.
observations: Every claim is tagged as Observed, Verified, or Inferred and no code was modified.
Only one live hook exists: a .claude/settings.json SessionStart command that runs a registry-freshness shell script.
That hook is non-blocking, always exits 0, and does not set COMPANY_SESSION_ID or fill channel registry entries.
No general event→action trigger engine exists; the word trigger appears only as descriptive fields or run-cause labels.
cc_board.file_item validates, atomically writes a record, and returns it without callbacks, notifies, enqueues, or emits.
The supervisor polls a durable mailbox file for intent records, not the noticeboard directory, and has no filesystem/inotify watch.
The board already has typed edges (authored_by, attached_to, sourced_from, promoted_from), stable board:// addresses, and inbound relations queries.
There is no reply-style edge kind such as responds_to in board_edges/.
The channel registry has description and profile fields, but the general channel.mcp.json startup only passes COMPANY_ROOT, leaving them empty.
Channel description/profile are filled only when an agent voluntarily calls announce or profile, prompted by prose instruction.
COMPANY_SESSION_ID is injected only on supervisor resume-spawn and operator clone launch; a code comment defers fresh/fork self-id to a SessionStart hook that does not exist.
The report ends with an inventory table of firing mechanisms and three verdicts.
standouts: The only live hook is the registry-freshness SessionStart script; the intended semantic-fill/self-id SessionStart hook is absent.
Board writes are inert: nothing watches the noticeboard and nothing is emitted on file_item.
The token trigger is descriptive everywhere; no code reads a trigger field to dispatch an action.
conventions: Tags evidence as Observed, Verified, or Inferred.
Cites exact file paths and line ranges for claims.
Uses section headers, tables, and verdict summaries.
Defines a clean primitive distinction between hook and trigger.
concerns: Dense line-cited scan that will stale quickly as source files change.
Mixes factual scan with design prescription and build-pack vocabulary.
Some negative conclusions rest on absence-of-evidence greps and docstring wording.
Long enough that maintaining it as a living document may be costly.
notes: A forensic scan arguing the codebase has exactly one harness hook and no general trigger engine, and that the missing SessionStart hook is why channel registration lacks semantic info and a self-session id.
questions: What exact env vars and registry entries was the missing SessionStart hook supposed to set?
How would a new responds_to edge relate to promoted_from and attached_to semantics?
What event source is intended to drive the built-but-not-armed activation substrate?
Is the proposed trigger layer meant to be registry-driven, hook-driven, or board-watch-driven?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated purpose is a read-only scan of hooks/events/board; it largely does that, but it also prescribes primitive definitions and build direction, so it is part scan and part design brief.
apparent_themes: Strict separation between harness hooks and application triggers.
Gaps between intended infrastructure and currently live wiring.
The board as a typed-link substrate ready for a reaction layer.
Agent discretion versus enforced automation in channel self-registration.
intention_signals: Establish a shared vocabulary (hook vs trigger) and justify adding a declarative event→action trigger layer, beginning with a SessionStart hook that fills channel semantic info and COMPANY_SESSION_ID.
novelty: The explicit framing that a single missing SessionStart hook is the root cause of both empty COMPANY_SESSION_ID and empty channel descriptions, plus the clean primitive distinction between hooks and triggers.
contribution: A detailed, evidence-tagged inventory of current hook/trigger/board wiring and specific gaps that a fused system can use to decide where to add event emission, a responds_to edge, and a semantic-fill SessionStart hook.
summary_for_embedding: A scan report mapping the company's existing hook, event, board, and channel-registry wiring, concluding only one SessionStart hook is live, no general trigger engine exists, board writes are inert, and an intended semantic-fill SessionStart hook is missing.
intention_for_embedding: To provide a factual baseline and shared vocabulary for designing a reusable event-driven trigger system and the missing SessionStart registration hook.
